notable.notable_blurb: America's longest-serving bank president at 1894 death

career.notes: Admitted bar 1830 Auburn NY. Formed firm SEWARD & BEARDSLEY with William H. Seward (later Lincoln's Secretary of State, Governor of NY 1839-43). When Seward became NY Governor, Beardsley partnered with John Porter. Judge Advocate on Seward's staff when Seward became brigadier general 1832.

President for 51 years — longest-serving bank president in the United States at his death. Also president of Oswego Starch Company (1883) and Auburn Savings Bank; director First National Bank of Auburn; original stockholder of multiple banks. Fortune estimated at $7 million at death.

narrative_blocks.header: America's longest-serving bank president at his death
narrative_blocks.body: He presided over Cayuga County National Bank in Auburn NY from 1843 until his death in 1894 — fifty-one years in the chair, the longest tenure of any bank president in the United States at the time. He simultaneously presided over Oswego Starch Company and Auburn Savings Bank. His fortune was estimated at seven million dollars at death, an extraordinary sum for an upstate NY lawyer.

narrative_blocks.header: Law partner of William Seward before Seward governed
narrative_blocks.body: Admitted to the bar in 1830, he formed the firm Seward & Beardsley in Auburn NY with the future Lincoln cabinet secretary and Alaska-Purchase architect. He stayed as judge advocate on Seward's staff when Seward was commissioned a brigadier general in 1832, then continued in Auburn law and banking when Seward moved to Albany as governor in 1839. The Seward connection anchored his political and business network for sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother Roswell was postmaster seventy-two years
narrative_blocks.body: His younger brother Roswell Beardsley served as postmaster at North Lansing NY for seventy-two consecutive years (1828-1902), under twenty US presidents — the longest postmaster tenure on record. A third brother William Clinton Beardsley was a Democratic presidential elector in 1852 and railroad director. The three Beardsley brothers held unusually long civic and business positions across upstate New York.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
